   "source": [
    "# Quickstart\n",
    "\n",
    "In this simple example we will generate some simulated data, and fit them with 3ML."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "0aa9456b",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Let's start by generating our dataset:\n"
   ]
  },

   "source": [
    "from jupyterthemes import jtplot\n",

    "jtplot.style(context=\"talk\", fscale=1, ticks=True, grid=False)\n",
    "silence_warnings()\n",
    "set_threeML_style()"
   ]
  },

   "source": [
    "# Let's generate some data with y = Powerlaw(x)\n",
    "\n",
    "gen_function = Powerlaw()\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "# Generate a dataset using the power law, and a\n",
    "# constant 30% error\n",
    "\n",
    "x = np.logspace(0, 2, 50)\n",
    "\n",
    "xyl_generator = XYLike.from_function(\n",
    "    \"sim_data\", function=gen_function, x=x, yerr=0.3 * gen_function(x)\n",
    ")\n",
    "\n",
    "y = xyl_generator.y\n",
    "y_err = xyl_generator.yerr"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "d720d9e5",
   "metadata": {},

       "    .dataframe thead th {\n",
       "        text-align: right;\n",
       "    }\n",
       "</style>\n",
       "<table border=\"1\" class=\"dataframe\">\n",
       "  <thead>\n",
       "    <tr style=\"text-align: right;\">\n",
       "      <th></th>\n",
       "      <th>statistical measures</th>\n",
       "    </tr>\n",
       "  </thead>\n",
       "  <tbody>\n",
       "    <tr>\n",
       "      <th>AIC</th>\n",
       "      <td>46.467712</td>\n",
       "    </tr>\n",
       "    <tr>\n",
       "      <th>BIC</th>\n",
       "      <td>50.036439</td>\n",
       "    </tr>\n",
       "  </tbody>\n",
       "</table>\n",
       "</div>"
      ],
      "text/plain": [
       "     statistical measures\n",
       "AIC             46.467712\n",
       "BIC             50.036439"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "fit_function = Powerlaw()\n",
    "\n",
    "xyl = XYLike(\"data\", x, y, y_err)\n",
    "\n",
    "results = xyl.fit(fit_function)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "1cd8132d",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Plot data and model:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 6,
   "id": "e4714265",
   "metadata": {
    "execution": {
     "iopub.execute_input": "2026-02-23T09:08:31.676615Z",
     "iopub.status.busy": "2026-02-23T09:08:31.676457Z",
     "iopub.status.idle": "2026-02-23T09:08:32.015538Z",
     "shell.execute_reply": "2026-02-23T09:08:32.014787Z"
    },
    "tags": [
     "nbsphinx-thumbnail"
    ]
   },
   "outputs": [
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 1040x910 with 1 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "fig = xyl.plot(x_scale=\"log\", y_scale=\"log\")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "6e2b0454",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Compute the goodness of fit using Monte Carlo simulations (NOTE: if you repeat this exercise from the beginning many time, you should find that the quantity \"gof\" is a random number distributed uniformly between 0 and 1. That is the expected result if the model is a good representation of the data)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 7,
   "id": "02cbe4d1",
   "metadata": {
    "execution": {
     "iopub.execute_input": "2026-02-23T09:08:32.016970Z",
     "iopub.status.busy": "2026-02-23T09:08:32.016823Z",
     "iopub.status.idle": "2026-02-23T09:09:18.762366Z",
     "shell.execute_reply": "2026-02-23T09:09:18.761616Z"
    }
   },
   "outputs": [
    {
     "data": {
      "application/vnd.jupyter.widget-view+json": {
       "model_id": "6e70ba76f42b414685ceea936820664e",
       "version_major": 2,
       "version_minor": 0
      },
      "text/plain": [
       "Goodness of fit computation:   0%|          | 0/1000 [00:00<?, ?it/s]"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "display_data"
    },
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "The null-hypothesis probability from simulations is 0.71\n"
     ]
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "gof, all_results, all_like_values = xyl.goodness_of_fit()\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"The null-hypothesis probability from simulations is %.2f\" % gof[\"data\"])"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "36f4143c",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The procedure outlined above works for any distribution for the data (Gaussian or Poisson). In this case we are using Gaussian data, thus the log(likelihood) is just half of a $\\chi^2$. We can then also use the $\\chi^2$ test, which gives a close result without performing simulations:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 8,
   "id": "441eb636",
   "metadata": {
    "execution": {
     "iopub.execute_input": "2026-02-23T09:09:18.763778Z",
     "iopub.status.busy": "2026-02-23T09:09:18.763609Z",
     "iopub.status.idle": "2026-02-23T09:09:18.768723Z",
     "shell.execute_reply": "2026-02-23T09:09:18.768073Z"
    }
   },
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "The null-hypothesis probability from theory is 0.71\n"
     ]
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "import scipy.stats\n",
    "\n",
    "# Retrieve the likelihood values\n",
    "like_values = results.get_statistic_frame()\n",
    "\n",
    "# Compute the number of degrees of freedom\n",
    "n_dof = len(xyl.x) - len(fit_function.free_parameters)\n",
    "\n",
    "# Get the observed value for chi2\n",
    "# (the factor of 2 comes from the fact that the Gaussian log-likelihood is half of a chi2)\n",
    "obs_chi2 = 2 * like_values[\"-log(likelihood)\"][\"data\"]\n",
    "\n",
    "theoretical_gof = scipy.stats.chi2(n_dof).sf(obs_chi2)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"The null-hypothesis probability from theory is %.2f\" % theoretical_gof)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "0df492c3",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "There are however many settings where a theoretical answer, such as the one provided by the $\\chi^2$ test, does not exist. A simple example is a fit where data follow the Poisson statistic. In that case, the MC computation can provide the answer."
   ]
  }
 ],
